## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

# jjezs_paddock ₣A-

## Goal

Windows test infrastructure for Recipe Bottle. Run RB's container isolation
tests on a Windows host with WSL, Cygwin, and dual Docker daemons.

## Current Design

The architectural backbone lives in BUS0 §Remote Node Access (`Tools/buk/vov_veiled/BUS0-BashUtilitiesSpec.adoc`)
plus three platform subdocs (`BUSNW-NodeWindows.adoc`, `BUSNL-NodeLinux.adoc`,
`BUSNM-NodeMac.adoc`). Read BUS0 §Remote Node Access first; this paddock
captures only state that doesn't fit there.

**Header decisions made in this heat:**

- BURH regime renamed to BURN (n = Node)
- BURN_TIER field added: `privileged | workload`
- Two-tier model:
  - Privileged tier: node-level ops (sshd config, user provisioning).
    Multi-resident allowed; one operator at a time by social contract.
  - Workload tier: user-level ops (normal remote work). Exclusive to one
    station user; ephemeral.
- Verb vocabulary (BUSN quoins): Garrison / Conscript / Discharge / Inventory
- Tabtarget colophon families under `buw-`:
  - `buw-rn{l,r,v}` — BURN regime ops (list / render / validate)
  - `buw-rnc` — SSH config aggregator (cross-tier, all profiles)
  - `buw-npg{l,m,c,w,p,x}` — Garrison per platform (privileged setup)
  - `buw-nwc{l,m,c,w,p,x}` — Conscript per platform (workload mint)
  - `buw-nw{d,i}` — Discharge / Inventory
  - `buw-nw{c,r,s}` — Check / Run / Ssh (workload operational)
  - `buw-hn0` — Handbook landing for node ops
- Visual proximity note: `buw-nwc` (Check) vs `buw-nwc{platform}` (Conscript)
  — distinct colophons, accepted

## Heat Sequence (post-triage and post-audit)

Spec backbone in place; spec subdocs need elaboration; implementation paces
slated; practice unchanged.

**Spec phase (pending):**

- ₢A-AAR specify-zburn-key-line-builder (reslated; BUWC half dropped)
- ₢A-AAQ elaborate-busnw-windows-ceremony (reslated; morphed from
  per-command subdocs)
- ₢A-AAU specify-busn-tier-refusal (newly slated)

**Implementation phase (newly slated, sequenced):**

- ₢A-AAV burh-to-burn-rename-buk (mechanical)
- ₢A-AAW burh-to-burn-rename-jjk (mechanical, depends on AAV)
- ₢A-AAX garrison-platform-machinery (refactor + new code, six platforms)
- ₢A-AAY conscript-discharge-inventory-machinery (workload-tier mirror,
  depends on AAX)
- ₢A-AAZ workload-operational-tabtargets (Check / Run / Ssh)
- ₢A-AAa node-handbook-landing-and-windows-residue (handbook restructure,
  depends on AAX and AAY)
- ₢A-AAb buw-rnc-ssh-config-aggregator (colophon move; may fold into AAV)

**Practice phase:**

- ₢A-AAC (reslated for new verbs) — practice with Garrison/Conscript on
  real hardware
- ₢A-AAD WSL + Cygwin install (unchanged)
- ₢A-AAE fundus provisioning (unchanged)
- ₢A-AAF Docker dual-daemon (unchanged)

**Regression:**

- ₢A-AAP retest fundus after practice (unchanged)

Triaged out (no slate change; act on at wrap time):

- ₢A-AAH (verification tabtargets) — fold into practice wrap notes
- ₢A-AAI (reconsider Cygwin install) — fold into AAD wrap notes
- ₢A-AAJ (autonumber sweep) — relocate to ₣A6
- ₢A-AAS, ₢A-AAT (handbook-render fixture coverage) — relocate to ₣BB

## Deferred (named for future return)

- **Adopt** — cross-station-user pubkey install via existing privileged
  trust. Today: each station user runs the ceremony fresh from their
  own machine.
- **User regime** — centralized per-user pubkey for shared admin
  scenarios. Pairs naturally with Adopt when both are needed.
- **Git normalization on remote** — JJK has `jjx_plant`; BUK doesn't
  duplicate.

## Standing Notes

- Tailscale provides stable transport (Mac sees `rocket` as
  `100.x.y.z` / tailnet hostname); not adopted as a BUK dependency
- Windows username `b hyslop` (with space) — audit shell quoting at
  every `${BURN_USER}` expansion during practice
- Fundus capability registry: `Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSFR-FundusRegistry.md`
- PowerShell-from-WSL pattern: bash owns control flow, PowerShell is
  the Windows syscall layer; one atomic command per call
- BURS_USER station regime field routes profiles to
  `.buk/users/${BURS_USER}/` subdirectory
- Three Windows BURN profiles for `rocket` distinguished by
  `BURN_COMMAND`: cygwin (`...bash.exe -l`), WSL (`wsl.exe -d ...`),
  PowerShell (`powershell.exe`)

# jjezs_pace ₢A-AAR

## Character

## Docket

This is the wire format for all key-install helpers across Garrison and Conscript. Marker format change breaks idempotency across all platforms.

content.text:
Honest read: I don't see a strong point either.

The pace is documenting the output format of one private helper. Look at what it actually specifies:
- A marker string (`# BURN:<alias>`)
- A key-line shape with optional `command=`
- Pure function, no I/O

This is ~30 lines of bash. The "wire format across Garrison and Conscript" framing makes it sound like a protocol between independent implementations — but Garrison and Conscript aren't independent; they both call the same helper. There's one implementation, not a contract between many.

The original AAR earned a spec pace because it included the BUWC module declaration — vocabulary that crossed module boundaries. That got cut under the tier model. What's left is implementation detail wearing a spec costume.

**Litmus test from CLAUDE.md** ("does this element earn its existence?"): a spec entry earns its keep when (a) multiple independent sites need to agree, or (b) the format outlives any single implementation, or (c) there's real design ambiguity worth pinning before code. None of those clearly apply here. The marker format is a 5-minute design call the implementer will make at the keyboard.

**Two ways to act on this:**

1. **Drop it** (`jjx_drop ₢A-AAR`) — let ₢A-AAX garrison-platform-machinery define the marker inline when the first helper lands. The format will document itself in code.

2. **Fold the question into AAX's docket** — note "decide marker format" as a sub-bullet so it's not lost, but kill the standalone spec pace.

I lean toward drop. If the marker format turns out to need cross-platform discipline beyond "one helper produces it, one greps for it," that's discoverable at implementation time and a future spec pace can capture it then with real evidence.

Want to drop it, fold it, or is there a reason you slated it that I'm missing?

### handbook-render-imprint-coverage (₢A-AAT) [rough]

## Character
Imprint-channel sibling to ₢A-AAS. Design decision pace — the core question is how `buh_tt` and the handbook-render fixture should treat imprint-channel colophons when the handbook is host-agnostic. Likely needs discussion before code lands.

## Docket
`buh_tt` delegates to `buyy_tt_yawp`, which globs `${BURD_TABTARGET_DIR}/${colophon}.*` when no imprint arg is supplied and picks the first alphabetical match. For imprint-channel colophons with multiple host variants, the first match is arbitrary.

### Concrete trigger
Surfaced during ₢A6AAn. In `Tools/rbk/rbh0/rbhw0_top.sh:52`, the Windows handbook teaches Phase 3 with:
```bash
buh_tt "     Phase 2 (clone repos):              " "${JJZ_FUNDUS_PHASE2}"
```
`JJZ_FUNDUS_PHASE2` is enrolled with channel `imprint`; the tabtargets are `jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.cerebro.sh` and `jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.localhost.sh`. The handbook is host-agnostic (it's about "a Windows host" in the abstract), so the glob picks `cerebro` — arbitrary and potentially misleading for a learner setting up a different host.

The prior hardcoded string was `tt/jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.{host}.sh` — explicit about the placeholder. The `buh_tt` replacement (prescribed in ₢A6AAn's docket) loses that teaching signal in exchange for a clickable link.

### Why this isn't caught by the fixture today
`handbook-render` checks exit 0, not rendered content correctness. The Windows handbook rendering with `cerebro.sh` baked in is a green case — the fixture catches display *breakage* but not display *drift*.

### Candidate directions
- **Suffix annotation idiom**: document the standard `buh_tt ... "${FOO}" "" " (pick your host)"` pattern for imprint-channel colophons in host-agnostic handbooks. Cheapest; no primitive change. Accepts that the rendered link points at an arbitrary variant.
- **Multi-variant rendering in `buyy_tt_yawp`**: when called with no imprint on an imprint-channel colophon, render something like `tt/jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.{host}.sh` (synthetic placeholder) instead of globbing. Needs a way to detect "colophon is imprint-channel" — likely requires `buz_exec_lookup` or a sibling query on the zipper registry.
- **Require explicit imprint**: make `buh_tt` on an imprint-channel colophon without an imprint arg a hard error. Forces every caller to make a choice. Breaking change; audit scope unclear without a grep sweep.
- **Companion fixture assertion**: extend `handbook-render` (coordinating with ₢A-AAS) to snapshot rendered content and diff against expected per-colophon output. Catches drift, not just breakage. Heavier but pairs naturally with the param1 coverage work.

### Prerequisites
- ₢A6AAn complete — it produced the concrete trigger.
- Likely best sequenced after ₢A-AAS so the fixture's parameterization mechanism is known before deciding whether to leverage it for content-snapshot testing.

### Wrap criterion
- Decision recorded on imprint-channel handling in handbooks (one of the directions above, or another).
- If a primitive change: `buh_tt`/`buyy_tt_yawp` updated, call sites audited, fixture updated if behavior is observable.
- If an idiom only: documented in BCG or the handbook-building guide, and `rbhw0_top.sh:52` updated to apply it.
- Windows handbook (`rbw-hw`) renders in a way that doesn't silently commit a learner to the wrong host variant.

### Notes
- Currently `JJZ_FUNDUS_PHASE2` is the only known imprint-channel colophon used from a host-agnostic handbook. `JJZ_FUNDUS_SCENARIO` and `JJZ_FUNDUS_SINGLE` are also imprint-channel but aren't referenced from handbooks at time of slate.
- Pairs thematically with ₢A-AAS — both address the gap where channeled colophons meet uniform handbook/fixture shapes. May be worth groom-time discussion of whether to merge.

### specify-zburn-key-line-builder (₢A-AAR) [rough]

## Character
Spec writing — single contract specification. Mechanical.

This is the wire format for all key-install helpers across Garrison and Conscript. Marker format change breaks idempotency across all platforms.

Garrison phases: console (operator at keyboard, handbook surface only), handshake (first SSH via temp creds), provisioning (sshd_config, service, firewall, admin authorized_keys), harden (disable temp auth, verify keys-only).

### handbook-render-param1-coverage (₢A-AAS) [rough]

## Character
Future follow-up to the handbook-render fixture landed in ₣A6 (₢A6AAt). Design decision pace — likely requires some discussion of how to represent per-colophon parameterization in Theurge's test fixtures before any code moves.

## Docket
The handbook-render fixture in ₣A6 exercises 15 of the 16 handbook colophons. The one skipped is `rbw-HWdw` (`RBZ_HW_DOCKER_WSL_NATIVE`), which uses the `param1` channel — it requires a WSL-target argument to run, and the fixture's uniform "invoke and check exit 0" pattern does not supply one.

Decide how the handbook-render fixture should handle this (and any future param1-channel handbooks). Candidate directions:

- **Per-colophon argument lookup**: extend the Rust-side handbook colophon registry with a `default_args` slice per entry. Most entries get `&[]`; `rbw-HWdw` gets a known-safe dummy. Keeps the case loop uniform, localizes the exception.
- **Dedicated subsection in the fixture**: a second section listing only param1 handbooks with their dummy values. Preserves the "one case per colophon" symmetry without threading argument tables through the uniform case.
- **Split fixture**: `handbook-render-simple` (no args) + `handbook-render-parameterized` (args explicit). Heavier and probably not worth it for one offender.

This pace picks a direction, implements it, and extends handbook-render coverage to `rbw-HWdw`.

### Prerequisites
- ₢A6AAt (handbook-render-fixture) must be complete — this pace patches a known scope deferral from that work.

### Wrap criterion
- handbook-render fixture exercises all 16 handbook colophons (including `rbw-HWdw`)
- Pattern generalizes to any future param1 handbooks added via `rbz_zipper.sh`
- Test asserts the chosen dummy param1 value(s) produce a rendering exit (not an argument-missing exit)

### Notes
- Known exit 0 is not the only valid verdict here. The test's purpose is to catch display breakage in handbook rendering — the dummy argument must steer toward a rendered output path, not an early-exit-due-to-bad-arg path.
- `rbw-HWdw` is currently the only param1 handbook. If a second one lands before this pace runs, reassess whether an inline table is still the right shape.

### handbook-render-imprint-coverage (₢A-AAT) [rough]

## Character
Imprint-channel sibling to ₢A-AAS. Design decision pace — the core question is how `buh_tt` and the handbook-render fixture should treat imprint-channel colophons when the handbook is host-agnostic. Likely needs discussion before code lands.

## Docket
`buh_tt` delegates to `buyy_tt_yawp`, which globs `${BURD_TABTARGET_DIR}/${colophon}.*` when no imprint arg is supplied and picks the first alphabetical match. For imprint-channel colophons with multiple host variants, the first match is arbitrary.

### Concrete trigger
Surfaced during ₢A6AAn. In `Tools/rbk/rbh0/rbhw0_top.sh:52`, the Windows handbook teaches Phase 3 with:
```bash
buh_tt "     Phase 2 (clone repos):              " "${JJZ_FUNDUS_PHASE2}"
```
`JJZ_FUNDUS_PHASE2` is enrolled with channel `imprint`; the tabtargets are `jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.cerebro.sh` and `jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.localhost.sh`. The handbook is host-agnostic (it's about "a Windows host" in the abstract), so the glob picks `cerebro` — arbitrary and potentially misleading for a learner setting up a different host.

The prior hardcoded string was `tt/jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.{host}.sh` — explicit about the placeholder. The `buh_tt` replacement (prescribed in ₢A6AAn's docket) loses that teaching signal in exchange for a clickable link.

### Why this isn't caught by the fixture today
`handbook-render` checks exit 0, not rendered content correctness. The Windows handbook rendering with `cerebro.sh` baked in is a green case — the fixture catches display *breakage* but not display *drift*.

### Candidate directions
- **Suffix annotation idiom**: document the standard `buh_tt ... "${FOO}" "" " (pick your host)"` pattern for imprint-channel colophons in host-agnostic handbooks. Cheapest; no primitive change. Accepts that the rendered link points at an arbitrary variant.
- **Multi-variant rendering in `buyy_tt_yawp`**: when called with no imprint on an imprint-channel colophon, render something like `tt/jjw-tfP2.ProvisionPhase2.{host}.sh` (synthetic placeholder) instead of globbing. Needs a way to detect "colophon is imprint-channel" — likely requires `buz_exec_lookup` or a sibling query on the zipper registry.
- **Require explicit imprint**: make `buh_tt` on an imprint-channel colophon without an imprint arg a hard error. Forces every caller to make a choice. Breaking change; audit scope unclear without a grep sweep.
- **Companion fixture assertion**: extend `handbook-render` (coordinating with ₢A-AAS) to snapshot rendered content and diff against expected per-colophon output. Catches drift, not just breakage. Heavier but pairs naturally with the param1 coverage work.

### Prerequisites
- ₢A6AAn complete — it produced the concrete trigger.
- Likely best sequenced after ₢A-AAS so the fixture's parameterization mechanism is known before deciding whether to leverage it for content-snapshot testing.

### Wrap criterion
- Decision recorded on imprint-channel handling in handbooks (one of the directions above, or another).
- If a primitive change: `buh_tt`/`buyy_tt_yawp` updated, call sites audited, fixture updated if behavior is observable.
- If an idiom only: documented in BCG or the handbook-building guide, and `rbhw0_top.sh:52` updated to apply it.
- Windows handbook (`rbw-hw`) renders in a way that doesn't silently commit a learner to the wrong host variant.

### Notes
- Currently `JJZ_FUNDUS_PHASE2` is the only known imprint-channel colophon used from a host-agnostic handbook. `JJZ_FUNDUS_SCENARIO` and `JJZ_FUNDUS_SINGLE` are also imprint-channel but aren't referenced from handbooks at time of slate.
- Pairs thematically with ₢A-AAS — both address the gap where channeled colophons meet uniform handbook/fixture shapes. May be worth groom-time discussion of whether to merge.

1283	== Remote Node Access
1284	
1285	Bringing remote nodes under SSH key control from a local station, establishing
1286	trust that supports both privileged node-level operations and ephemeral
1287	workload-tier work over the same {burn_regime}.
1288	
1289	=== Privilege Tiers
1290	
1291	The {burn_tier} field of every {burn_regime} profile classifies the profile
1292	as either `privileged` or `workload`. Tabtargets refuse aliases of the wrong
1293	tier at entry.
1294	
1295	==== Privileged Tier
1296	
1297	Privileged profiles authenticate as a remote OS user with admin or equivalent
1298	privileges. Used for node-level operations: sshd configuration, user
1299	provisioning, node-establishment ceremonies.
1300	
1301	Multiple station users may hold privileged trust on the same node (file
1302	format supports multi-resident keys). Operational convention: only one
1303	station user actively orchestrates work at a time. This convention is
1304	enforced by social contract, not BUK locking.
1305	
1306	==== Workload Tier
1307	
1308	Workload profiles authenticate as ephemeral remote OS users created by
1309	privileged authority. Used for normal remote work: running commands,
1310	file operations, interactive shells.
1311	
1312	Workload users are exclusive to one station user — not handed off between
1313	station users. Lifecycle is short:
1314	{busn_conscript}
1315	when needed,
1316	{busn_discharge}
1317	when done.
1318	
1319	=== Tabtarget Catalog
1320	
1321	Tabtargets under `buw-` consume {burn_regime} profiles to perform remote
1322	operations. Tier-specific colophons follow `buw-n[pw]` for clarity.
1323	
1324	==== Lifecycle
1325	
1326	`buw-rnl`::
1327	List all {burn_regime} profiles in the local station subtree.
1328	
1329	`buw-rnr <alias>`::
1330	Render a single {burn_regime} profile.
1331	
1332	`buw-rnv <alias>`::
1333	Validate a single {burn_regime} profile.
1334	
1335	==== Privileged (per-platform node setup)
1336	
1337	`buw-npg{l,m,c,w,p,x} <host> <user> <moniker>`::
1338	{busn_garrison} a node for the named platform.
1339	Writes the privileged {burn_regime} profile and runs the platform-specific
1340	ceremony to establish admin trust.
1341	Platform letters: l=Linux, m=Mac, c=Cygwin, w=WSL, p=PowerShell, x=Localhost.
1342	
1343	==== Workload (privileged authority required)
1344	
1345	`buw-nwc{l,m,c,w,p,x} <privileged-alias> <workload-moniker>`::
1346	{busn_conscript} a workload user via the named privileged BURN.
1347	Creates the remote OS user, installs the station user's pubkey, and
1348	writes a workload {burn_regime} profile locally.
1349	
1350	`buw-nwd <workload-alias>`::
1351	{busn_discharge} a workload user.
1352	Deletes the remote OS user via the workload profile's privileged
1353	authority and removes the local profile.
1354	
1355	`buw-nwi <privileged-alias>`::
1356	{busn_inventory} workload users present on the node, queried via the
1357	privileged BURN.
1358	
1359	==== Workload (operational)
1360	
1361	`buw-nwc <workload-alias>`::
1362	Check responsiveness via passwordless SSH probe.
1363	
1364	`buw-nwr <workload-alias> <cmd-file>`::
1365	Run the contents of `<cmd-file>` on the remote node; capture output to

== Remote Node Access

Bringing remote nodes under SSH key control from a local station, establishing
trust that supports both privileged node-level operations and ephemeral
workload-tier work over the same {burn_regime}.

=== Privilege Tiers

The {burn_tier} field of every {burn_regime} profile classifies the profile
as either `privileged` or `workload`. Tabtargets refuse aliases of the wrong
tier at entry.

==== Privileged Tier

Privileged profiles authenticate as a remote OS user with admin or equivalent
privileges. Used for node-level operations: sshd configuration, user
provisioning, node-establishment ceremonies.

Multiple station users may hold privileged trust on the same node (file
format supports multi-resident keys). Operational convention: only one
station user actively orchestrates work at a time. This convention is
enforced by social contract, not BUK locking.

==== Workload Tier

Workload profiles authenticate as ephemeral remote OS users created by
privileged authority. Used for normal remote work: running commands,
file operations, interactive shells.

Workload users are exclusive to one station user — not handed off between
station users. Lifecycle is short:
{busn_conscript}
when needed,
{busn_discharge}
when done.

=== Tabtarget Catalog

Tabtargets under `buw-` consume {burn_regime} profiles to perform remote
operations. Tier-specific colophons follow `buw-n[pw]` for clarity.

==== Lifecycle

`buw-rnl`::
List all {burn_regime} profiles in the local station subtree.

`buw-rnr <alias>`::
Render a single {burn_regime} profile.

`buw-rnv <alias>`::
Validate a single {burn_regime} profile.

==== Privileged (per-platform node setup)

`buw-npg{l,m,c,w,p,x} <host> <user> <moniker>`::
{busn_garrison} a node for the named platform.
Writes the privileged {burn_regime} profile and runs the platform-specific
ceremony to establish admin trust.
Platform letters: l=Linux, m=Mac, c=Cygwin, w=WSL, p=PowerShell, x=Localhost.

==== Workload (privileged authority required)

`buw-nwc{l,m,c,w,p,x} <privileged-alias> <workload-moniker>`::
{busn_conscript} a workload user via the named privileged BURN.
Creates the remote OS user, installs the station user's pubkey, and
writes a workload {burn_regime} profile locally.

`buw-nwd <workload-alias>`::
{busn_discharge} a workload user.
Deletes the remote OS user via the workload profile's privileged
authority and removes the local profile.

`buw-nwi <privileged-alias>`::
{busn_inventory} workload users present on the node, queried via the
privileged BURN.

==== Workload (operational)

`buw-nwc <workload-alias>`::
Check responsiveness via passwordless SSH probe.

`buw-nwr <workload-alias> <cmd-file>`::
Run the contents of `<cmd-file>` on the remote node; capture output to
{burd_output_dir}.

`buw-nws <workload-alias>`::
Open an interactive SSH session.

==== Handbook

`buw-hn0`::
Top-level handbook landing for node access procedures.

=== Privileges

The {burs_user} owns its profile subtree at `.buk/users/{burs_user}/`
exclusively — operations write only under the current station user's tree.

A {burn_regime} profile describes a connection identity (host, user, shell,
alias, key, tier) — not project state, not work-directory. Project context
is supplied by the consumer at invocation time.

Each platform subdoc declares the remote-user privilege contract that its
ceremony assumes (e.g., on Windows the privileged user must be a member of
the Administrators group).

=== Verb Definitions

[[busn_garrison]]
//axl_voices axt_command
{busn_garrison}::
Establish privileged BURN trust on a node by writing the privileged
{burn_regime} profile and running the platform-specific ceremony to gain
admin access. Per-platform tabtargets at `buw-npg{platform}`. First-time
node setup; idempotent on re-run.

[[busn_conscript]]
//axl_voices axt_command
{busn_conscript}::
Create an ephemeral workload user on a node and provision a workload
{burn_regime} profile locally. Requires a privileged BURN as authority for
the remote-user creation. Per-platform tabtargets at `buw-nwc{platform}`,
where the platform letter selects the workload shell — independent of the
privileged BURN's shell choice.

[[busn_discharge]]
//axl_voices axt_command
{busn_discharge}::
Remove a workload user from a node and delete the local workload
{burn_regime} profile. The workload profile encodes its privileged
authority; {busn_discharge} uses that authority to delete the remote OS
user. Tabtarget: `buw-nwd`.

[[busn_inventory]]
//axl_voices axt_command
{busn_inventory}::
List workload users currently provisioned on a node, queried via a
privileged BURN. Scope is the node's full workload-user population — not
just the current station user's history. Tabtarget: `buw-nwi`.

include::BUSNW-NodeWindows.adoc[]

include::BUSNL-NodeLinux.adoc[]

include::BUSNM-NodeMac.adoc[]

[[burn_regime]]
//axvr_regime axf_bash axrd_file_sourced
{burn_regime}::
Per-user, per-connection-profile regime for SSH access to remote nodes.
Unlike singleton regimes, BURN is multi-instance: one assignment file per SSH connection,
stored at `.buk/users/{burs_user}/<alias>/burn.env`.
Each profile describes one SSH connection — one key, one alias, one entry point —
classified by {burn_tier} as either privileged (node-level operations) or workload
(user-level operations on an established node).

[[burn_host]]
//axvr_variable axd_required axtu_string
{burn_host}::
IP address or hostname of the remote node.

[[burn_user]]
//axvr_variable axd_required axtu_xname
{burn_user}::
Username on the remote node.
May differ from the local
{burs_user}.

[[burn_alias]]
//axvr_variable axd_required axtu_xname
{burn_alias}::
SSH alias and key filename.
Must match the directory name containing this profile's assignment file.

[[burn_ssh_pubkey]]
//axvr_variable axd_required axtu_string
{burn_ssh_pubkey}::
Full SSH public key line (e.g., `ssh-ed25519 AAAA... user@host`).
Git-transported: no private key material.
Used by handbook procedures to render `authorized_keys` entries and `ssh-keygen` commands.

[[burn_key_file]]
//axvr_variable axd_optional axtu_string
{burn_key_file}::
SSH private key filename within `~/.ssh/`.
Empty defaults to {burn_alias} (one key per connection).
Localhost profiles set `id_ed25519` to share the invoking user's default key.

[[burn_command]]
//axvr_variable axd_required axtu_string
{burn_command}::
Shell command for `command=` routing in SSH `authorized_keys`.
Empty for direct-shell connections (Linux, macOS, localhost — no routing needed).
When set, determines which shell or environment the connection launches
(e.g., `C:\cygwin64\bin\bash.exe -l` for Cygwin, `wsl.exe -d Ubuntu` for WSL).

[[burn_tier]]
//axvr_variable axd_required axtu_xname
{burn_tier}::
Privilege classification of this profile.
Values: `privileged` (admin-level operations on the node — sshd configuration,
user provisioning, node-level setup) or `workload` (normal remote work as an
ephemeral user on an established node).

refuse workload aliases and vice versa.

=== Windows Node Access

Platform-specific ceremony and contracts for Windows node access.

==== Status

Stub.
Elaborate content to be drafted by subsequent paces — Windows is the
canonical worked example for the Remote Node Access ceremony, requiring
detailed coverage of OpenSSH server provisioning, the
`administrators_authorized_keys` special case, ACL fixes via `icacls`,
and the temp-password bootstrap handshake.

==== Privilege Contract

The {burn_user} of a Windows privileged BURN must be a member of the local
Administrators group.
Rationale: Windows OpenSSH consults `C:\ProgramData\ssh\administrators_authorized_keys`
for users in the Administrators group, ignoring the per-user
`~/.ssh/authorized_keys` for those users.
A non-admin path would require parallel implementation with no return for
the supported use cases.

Asserted at every ceremony participant before any modification.

==== Platform Letters

* `c` — Cygwin (`buw-npgc`, `buw-nwcc`)
* `w` — WSL (`buw-npgw`, `buw-nwcw`)
* `p` — PowerShell (`buw-npgp`, `buw-nwcp`)

==== Out of Band

The console phase of the Windows ceremony is performed at the Windows
physical keyboard (or RDP) by the operator and is described in the BUK
handbook surface, not in this spec.

# jjezs_reslate A-AAV

## Character
Mechanical sweep — rename across BUK, no semantic change. Absorbs former AAU (tier-refusal helper) and AAb (SSH config aggregator colophon move).

## Docket
Wholesale rename BURH → BURN across BUK: variables, function prefixes, module file names, profile filename (`burh.env` → `burn.env`), zipper enrollments, tabtarget filenames. Add `BURN_TIER` enrollment (`privileged|workload`); the field exists, enforcement helper lands here.

Constructor enrollments (`BUWZ_RHC_*`) and Windows command enrollments (`BUWZ_WC_*`) keep names provisionally; they retire in Garrison/Conscript paces, not here.

Tier-refusal helper: mint a centralized assertion (e.g., `burn_assert_tier <expected> <alias>`) that compares the alias's `BURN_TIER` against the expected tier. Error format: name expected tier, observed tier, alias name; exit non-zero before any side effect. BUS0 already states the contract on `burn_tier`; this codifies the helper and its error shape so Garrison/Conscript paces consume one implementation.

SSH config aggregator move: rename `buw-HWsc.SshConfig.sh` → `buw-rnc.NodeSshConfig.sh` and update its underlying function/module names. Tier-aware filtering: aggregator emits `~/.ssh/config` entries for both tiers (privileged and workload); document the choice. Update handbook references that pointed at the old colophon.

Validation: `tt/buw-st.BukSelfTest.sh` and BUK-touching fixtures pass.

Discovery: `grep -rln 'BURH\|burh' Tools/buk/ tt/`.

# jjezs_reslate A-AAX

## Character
Implementation — net new code with refactor absorbing existing constructor + Windows-specific provisioning into per-platform routines. Multi-shell decomposition for Windows needs design judgment. Defines the key-line wire format inline.

## Docket
Mint `busn_garrison` per platform per BUS0 catalog (`buw-npg{l,m,c,w,p,x}`). Each writes a privileged `burn.env` (BURN_TIER=privileged) and runs the platform ceremony.

Linux/Mac/Localhost: minimal — assume existing sshd, key install via privileged authority.

Windows variants (Cygwin/WSL/PowerShell): full ceremony — sshd bootstrap, admin authorized_keys install with `command=` routing per shell. Console phase is handbook-surface residue (later pace), not Garrison code.

Key-line wire format (the former AAR scope, now inline): the renamed `zburn_build_key_line` helper produces `[command="...",options ]<keytype> <base64> # BURN:<alias>` — the trailing `# BURN:<alias>` is the idempotency marker that key-install and SSH-config-aggregation logic grep for. The `command=` prefix is conditional on the platform's routing needs (Windows shells require it; direct-shell platforms omit it). Keep the format definition co-located with the only producer.

Retire `buw-rhc{c,l,m,p,w,x}`, `buw-wcb`, `buw-wck` files and zipper enrollments.

Tier assertion via the helper minted in AAV: Garrison rejects workload aliases.

# jjezs_reslate A-AAP

## Character
Test execution — regression check after practice-driven amendments.

## Docket
Re-run the full fundus scenario test suite (`tt/jjw-tfs.TestFundusScenario.localhost.sh`) to confirm that any code changes made during practice paces (AAC–AAF) haven't broken the BURN-based bind path.

Same coverage as ₢A-AAO but exercised after practice amendments have landed. If failures surface, fix and iterate.

Implementation — net new code with refactor absorbing existing constructor + Windows-specific provisioning into per-platform routines. Multi-shell decomposition for Windows needs design judgment. Defines the key-line wire format inline.

## Docket
Mint `busn_garrison` per platform per BUS0 catalog (`buw-npg{l,m,c,w,p,x}`). Each writes a privileged `burn.env` (BURN_TIER=privileged) and runs the platform ceremony.

Linux/Mac/Localhost: minimal — assume existing sshd, key install via privileged authority.

Windows variants (Cygwin/WSL/PowerShell): full ceremony — sshd bootstrap, admin authorized_keys install with `command=` routing per shell. Console phase is handbook-surface residue (later pace), not Garrison code.

Key-line wire format (the former AAR scope, now inline): the renamed `zburn_build_key_line` helper produces `[command="...",options ]<keytype> <base64> # BURN:<alias>` — the trailing `# BURN:<alias>` is the idempotency marker that key-install and SSH-config-aggregation logic grep for. The `command=` prefix is conditional on the platform's routing needs (Windows shells require it; direct-shell platforms omit it). Keep the format definition co-located with the only producer.

Retire `buw-rhc{c,l,m,p,w,x}`, `buw-wcb`, `buw-wck` files and zipper enrollments.

Tier assertion via the helper minted in AAV: Garrison rejects workload aliases.

# jjezs_reslate A-AAP

## Character
Test execution — regression check after practice-driven amendments.

## Docket
Re-run the full fundus scenario test suite (`tt/jjw-tfs.TestFundusScenario.localhost.sh`) to confirm that any code changes made during practice paces (AAC–AAF) haven't broken the BURN-based bind path.

Same coverage as ₢A-AAO but exercised after practice amendments have landed. If failures surface, fix and iterate.

# jjezs_paddock ₣A-

## Goal

Windows test infrastructure for Recipe Bottle. Run RB's container isolation
tests on a Windows host with WSL, Cygwin, and dual Docker daemons.

## Current Design

The architectural backbone lives in BUS0 §Remote Node Access (`Tools/buk/vov_veiled/BUS0-BashUtilitiesSpec.adoc`)
plus three platform subdocs (`BUSNW-NodeWindows.adoc`, `BUSNL-NodeLinux.adoc`,
`BUSNM-NodeMac.adoc`). Read BUS0 §Remote Node Access first; this paddock
captures only state that doesn't fit there.

**Header decisions made in this heat:**

- BURH regime renamed to BURN (n = Node)
- BURN_TIER field added: `privileged | workload`
- Two-tier model:
  - Privileged tier: node-level ops (sshd config, user provisioning).
    Multi-resident allowed; one operator at a time by social contract.
  - Workload tier: user-level ops (normal remote work). Exclusive to one
    station user; ephemeral.
- Verb vocabulary (BUSN quoins): Garrison / Conscript / Discharge / Inventory
- Tabtarget colophon families under `buw-`:
  - `buw-rn{l,r,v}` — BURN regime ops (list / render / validate)
